items.subtopic: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS OF APPENDICITIS AND APPENDICEAL PERFORATION
items.title: Incidence and Risk Factors of Acute Appendicitis
items.content: Acute appendicitis is a common surgical emergency with an incidence rate of approximately 10–11 cases per 10,000 people annually. It predominantly affects individuals aged 10-19 years, though the average age at diagnosis is gradually increasing. The risk of perforation stands at ~10–20%, significantly higher in patients under 5 or over 65 years old. Socioeconomic status inversely correlates with appendicitis risk.
keyPoints: Incidence rate: ~10–11 cases per 10,000 people annually
Common age group: 10-19 years
Risk of perforation: ~10–20%
Higher risk in patients <5 or >65 years old
Lower appendicitis risk with higher socioeconomic status
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS OF APPENDICITIS AND APPENDICEAL PERFORATION

items.subtopic: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS OF APPENDICITIS AND APPENDICEAL PERFORATION
items.title: Pathogenesis of Appendicitis and Factors Leading to Perforation
items.content: The pathogenesis of appendicitis involves obstruction of the appendiceal lumen, leading to bacterial overgrowth, increased intraluminal pressure, vascular thrombosis, ischemic necrosis, and potential perforation. Fecaliths are found in ~50% of patients with gangrenous appendicitis who perforate, suggesting different pathophysiologic processes compared to simpler cases.
keyPoints: Obstruction believed to be a key step in appendicitis development
Fecaliths present in ~50% of perforating gangrenous appendicitis cases
Perforation risk varies based on underlying pathophysiology
Uncomplicated appendicitis may resolve or require appendectomy
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS OF APPENDICITIS AND APPENDICEAL PERFORATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Early Identification of Appendicitis
items.content: Appendicitis should be considered in the differential diagnosis for any patient with abdominal pain unless appendectomy has been confirmed. Early identification is crucial, as persistent symptoms beyond 48 hours increase the risk of perforation and complications.
keyPoints: Appendicitis can present atypically; consider it unless appendectomy is confirmed.
Persistent symptoms over 48 hours may indicate higher risk for complications.
<1% mortality rate with improved diagnosis, care, and interventions.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Anatomic Variability of the Appendix
items.content: The appendix's location can vary, affecting clinical presentation. It may be found in different quadrants or even on the left side due to malrotation or redundant colon.
keyPoints: Appendix location varies: right lower quadrant, pelvis, right flank, right upper quadrant (pregnancy), or left side (malrotation).
Location influences presentation; consider atypical locations in diagnosis.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Considerations
items.content: Diagnosing appendicitis is challenging due to its broad differential diagnosis. Symptoms may not follow the classic pattern, especially in atypical locations or specific populations like women of childbearing age.
keyPoints: Classic symptoms: initial epigastric pain migrating to right lower quadrant over 12-24 hours.
Nausea and emesis typically follow abdominal pain onset.
Anorexia is common; absence may question appendicitis diagnosis.
Rectal examination is essential for all patients.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Rovsing's Sign in Appendicitis
items.content: Rovsing's sign is a clinical indicator of appendicitis, where gentle pressure applied to the left lower quadrant elicits pain in the right lower quadrant. This occurs due to peritoneal irritation when the appendix is located in the right lower quadrant.
keyPoints: Maximal tenderness often at McBurney's point, one-third along a line from the anterior iliac spine to the umbilicus.
Rovsing's sign: pain in the right lower quadrant upon left lower quadrant pressure.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Atypical Presentation of Appendicitis in Different Age Groups
items.content: Appendicitis can present atypically, especially in very young and elderly patients. Children may show dramatic responses to stimulation and have difficulty with history-taking due to their age. The smaller omentum in children might not effectively wall off an appendiceal perforation. In the elderly, symptoms like pain may be minimal or absent, with nausea, anorexia, and emesis being more prominent.
keyPoints: Atypical presentations are common in very young and elderly patients.
Children may have dramatic responses to stimulation; smaller omentum less likely to wall off perforation.
Elderly may exhibit minimal pain, with nausea, anorexia, and emesis as predominant complaints.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Laboratory Testing in Appendicitis: Limited Diagnostic Value
items.content: Laboratory tests, including white blood cell count and urinalysis, are not definitive for diagnosing appendicitis. While leukocytosis occurs in ~70% of simple appendicitis cases with counts between 10,000–18,000 cells/μL, it is insufficient for diagnosis. Urinalysis may help exclude genitourinary conditions but can show nonspecific findings like sterile pyuria or hematuria.
keyPoints: White blood cell count mildly to moderately elevated in ~70% of simple appendicitis cases (10,000–18,000 cells/μL).
A 'left shift' toward immature polymorphonuclear leukocytes is present in >95% of cases.
Urinalysis can exclude genitourinary conditions but may show nonspecific findings such as sterile pyuria or hematuria.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · LABORATORY TESTING | IMAGING

items.title: Imaging Modalities for Appendicitis: Sensitivity and Specificity Considerations
items.content: Ultrasonography, with a sensitivity of ~0.86 and specificity of 0.81, is operator-dependent and may not visualize the appendix even in skilled hands. CT imaging offers higher diagnostic accuracy with a sensitivity and specificity of at least 0.94 and 0.95, respectively. It is particularly useful when diagnosis is uncertain or to assess severity without peritoneal signs.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography has a sensitivity of ~0.86 and specificity of 0.81; findings include wall thickening, increased diameter, and free fluid.
CT imaging offers higher accuracy with sensitivity and specificity of at least 0.94 and 0.95.
Nonvisualization of the appendix on CT is nonspecific and should not rule out appendicitis.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · LABORATORY TESTING | IMAGING

items.title: Diagnosing Appendicitis During Pregnancy: Challenges and Risks
items.content: Appendicitis is the most common surgical emergency in pregnant patients. The diagnosis can be challenging due to anatomical changes from the enlarging uterus, which may push the appendix higher or obscure typical physical findings. Ultrasonography aids early diagnosis, but a high index of suspicion is crucial because untreated appendicitis significantly increases fetal mortality rates.
keyPoints: Appendicitis is the most common extrauterine surgical emergency during pregnancy.
Uterus enlargement can push the appendix higher or obscure physical findings.
Fetal mortality rate increases fourfold (5% to 20%) with appendiceal perforation.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · SPECIAL PATIENT POPULATIONS | Acute Appendicitis

items.title: Management of Appendicitis in Immunocompromised Patients: Considerations and Imaging
items.content: Immunocompromised patients may present with mild symptoms, complicating the diagnosis of appendicitis. They might have atypical infections or enterocolitis due to chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. CT imaging is helpful but should not delay surgical intervention if appendicitis is suspected.
keyPoints: Mild tenderness and atypical infections can complicate appendicitis diagnosis in immunocompromised patients.
CT imaging aids diagnosis but should not delay surgery for suspected appendicitis.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · SPECIAL PATIENT POPULATIONS | Acute Appendicitis

items.title: Understanding Etiology: Differentiating Secondary from Primary Peritonitis
items.content: Acute peritonitis can be classified as secondary or primary based on the presence of an identifiable intraabdominal source. Secondary peritonitis often results from perforation of a hollow viscus, such as the appendix, colonic diverticula, stomach, or duodenum, and is commonly associated with infective organisms. In contrast, primary peritonitis occurs without an identifiable intraabdominal source, frequently in patients with ascites or hypoproteinemia (<1 g/L).
keyPoints: Secondary peritonitis often results from perforation of the appendix, colonic diverticula, stomach, or duodenum.
Primary peritonitis occurs without an identifiable intraabdominal source and is common in patients with ascites or hypoproteinemia (<1 g/L).
Over 90% of primary peritonitis cases occur in these specific patient populations.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · ACUTE PERITONITIS | ETIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | THERAPY AND PROGNOSIS

items.title: Therapeutic Approach: Mortality Rates and Treatment Strategies for Peritonitis
items.content: The prognosis of acute peritonitis varies significantly based on patient health status. Mortality rates can be less than 10% in healthy patients with localized peritonitis, but exceed 40% in elderly or immunocompromised individuals. Effective treatment involves correcting electrolyte imbalances, restoring fluid volume and cardiovascular stability, administering appropriate antibiotics, and surgically addressing any underlying issues.
keyPoints: Mortality rates <10% for healthy patients with localized peritonitis.
Mortality rates >40% in elderly or immunocompromised patients.
Treatment includes correcting electrolyte imbalances, restoring fluid volume, stabilizing cardiovascular system, appropriate antibiotic therapy, and surgical correction.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · ACUTE PERITONITIS | ETIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | THERAPY AND PROGNOSIS

items.question: Where is maximal tenderness classically identified in patients with appendicitis?
options: Right lower quadrant at or near McBurney's point
items.explanation: Maximal tenderness is typically found in the right lower quadrant at or near McBurney's point.

items.explanation: Gentle pressure in the left lower quadrant may elicit pain in the right lower quadrant, known as Rovsing's sign.

Presence of red or white blood cells on urinalysis
Leukocytosis with a count of 10,000–18,000 cells/μL

items.question: Which imaging finding is not consistent with a diagnosis of appendicitis?
options: "Lumen that does not fill with enteric contrast"
"Fatty tissue stranding or air surrounding the appendix"

items.question: In immunocompromised patients, which of the following may be a concern when presenting with abdominal pain and fever?

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Patients with appendicitis often present with nausea before abdominal pain.
items.explanation: In appendicitis, nausea follows the development of abdominal pain.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: A pelvic examination is mandatory in women to rule out conditions that mimic appendicitis.
items.explanation: Pelvic examinations are necessary to exclude urogynecologic causes of abdominal pain.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: In children, the smaller omentum is more likely to wall off an appendiceal perforation.
items.explanation: The smaller omentum in children may be less likely to wall off an appendiceal perforation.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Eliciting signs of parietal peritoneal irritation can involve gentle abdominal percussion.
items.explanation: Gentle abdominal percussion is a method to elicit evidence of parietal peritoneal irritation.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Plain films of the abdomen are routinely obtained in suspected cases of acute appendicitis.
items.explanation: "Plain films of the abdomen are rarely helpful and so are not routinely obtained unless the clinician is worried about other conditions."

items.statement: Nonvisualization of the appendix on CT scan should be used to rule out appendicitis.

items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · LABORATORY TESTING | IMAGING

items.statement: Laparoscopic appendectomy is not recommended for patients with uncomplicated appendicitis.
items.explanation: Both laparoscopic and open appendectomy are satisfactory choices for patients with uncomplicated appendicitis.

items.statement: The mortality rate for uncomplicated, nonperforated appendicitis is 0.1-0.5%.

items.statement: Bowel sounds are a reliable physical finding for diagnosing peritonitis.

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Patients with appendicitis typically present with a temperature >38.3°C.
items.reason: A higher temperature and rigors indicate complications like perforation or abscess formation.
items.explanation: Patients with simple appendicitis usually have only slightly elevated temperatures.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: The appendix's anatomic location does not affect the clinical presentation of appendicitis.
items.reason: Variations in the appendix's position can influence how symptoms present and where pain is localized.
items.explanation: The anatomic location of the appendix directly influences the patient's presentation.
items.reference: Acute Appendicitis and Peritonitis · TABLE 342-1 Some Conditions That Mimic Appendicitis | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Diagnosing appendicitis in children can be challenging.
items.reason: Children may respond dramatically to stimulation and obtaining an accurate history is difficult.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why diagnosing appendicitis in children is challenging.
